content.text: FlashAttention's performance relies on two intertwined techniques: tiling and recomputation. Tiling breaks the N×N attention computation into small blocks that fit in on-chip SRAM, eliminating HBM round-trips for intermediate results. Recomputation allows the backward pass to regenerate those intermediate results on the fly from compact softmax statistics — avoiding the need to store the N×N attention matrix as a training activation. Neither technique works without the other: tiling produces the running statistics (
) that make recomputation exact, and recomputation is what makes storing those statistics (rather than the full N×N matrix) sufficient for training.
(column block, over K and V) are constrained by SRAM capacity. At any moment, the SRAM must hold:
The binding SRAM constraint (excluding the transient

tokens. In practice the FlashAttention CUDA kernel uses tuned block sizes (typically 64 or 128) that maximize throughput accounting for register pressure and warp occupancy. Larger tiles improve arithmetic intensity (more compute per HBM byte loaded) but require more registers per warp, reducing SM occupancy.

attrs.title: FlashAttention Forward Pass: Tiling the N×N Attention Matrix

In causal (autoregressive) attention, the kernel skips all tiles where
— no computation is wasted on masked-out positions. Of the
total tiles, only the lower-triangular half (approximately

tiles) are computed, plus the diagonal tiles with partial masking. This means causal FlashAttention has roughly half the FLOPs of bidirectional FlashAttention — and for prefill of decoder models, this halves the compute cost:
Standard backpropagation through attention requires the softmax output
to compute gradients. The backward pass needs P to compute
and the gradient through the softmax Jacobian. Standard attention stores P as an N×N activation, requiring O(N²) memory.
FlashAttention's backward pass instead recomputes each
tile of P on demand using the forward pass inputs (Q, K — already in HBM for gradient computation) and the stored statistics (
is the running maximum that was used to normalize the final softmax, and
is the exact denominator. The recomputed
tile is bit-identical to what the forward pass would have materialized for that tile — without ever writing it to HBM during the forward pass.
The following single-head Python reference implements both the forward tiling and the backward recomputation, making the relationship between stored statistics and recomputed tiles explicit.

import torch, math

def flash_attn_forward(
    q: torch.Tensor,   # (N, d)
    k: torch.Tensor,   # (N, d)
    v: torch.Tensor,   # (N, d)
    block_size: int = 64,
    causal: bool = True,
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """Returns output O, running max m, running sum ℓ."""
    N, d = q.shape
    scale = d ** -0.5
    Tr = math.ceil(N / block_size)
    Tc = math.ceil(N / block_size)

    O = torch.zeros_like(q)
    m = torch.full((N,), float("-inf"), dtype=q.dtype, device=q.device)
    l = torch.zeros(N, dtype=q.dtype, device=q.device)

    for i in range(Tr):
        r0, r1 = i * block_size, min((i + 1) * block_size, N)
        qi = q[r0:r1]                    # load Q_i into "SRAM"
        oi = torch.zeros_like(qi)        # O accumulator
        mi = torch.full((r1 - r0,), float("-inf"), dtype=q.dtype, device=q.device)
        li = torch.zeros(r1 - r0, dtype=q.dtype, device=q.device)

        for j in range(Tc):
            c0, c1 = j * block_size, min((j + 1) * block_size, N)
            if causal and c0 >= r1:
                break  # all future tiles — skip

            kj = k[c0:c1]                # load K_j
            vj = v[c0:c1]                # load V_j
            sij = (qi @ kj.T) * scale   # (B_r, B_c) — transient, never stored

            if causal:
                row_idx = torch.arange(r0, r1, device=q.device).unsqueeze(1)
                col_idx = torch.arange(c0, c1, device=q.device).unsqueeze(0)
                sij = sij.masked_fill(col_idx > row_idx, float("-inf"))

            # Online softmax update (all in SRAM)
            mij_local = sij.max(dim=-1).values
            mi_new = torch.maximum(mi, mij_local)

            exp_sij = torch.exp(sij - mi_new.unsqueeze(1))  # (B_r, B_c)
            rescale = torch.exp(mi - mi_new)                  # (B_r,)

            li = rescale * li + exp_sij.sum(dim=-1)
            oi = rescale.unsqueeze(1) * oi + exp_sij @ vj
            mi = mi_new

        # Finalize and write O_i, m_i, l_i to "HBM"
        O[r0:r1] = oi / li.unsqueeze(1)
        m[r0:r1] = mi
        l[r0:r1] = li

    return O, m, l


def flash_attn_backward_recompute(
    q: torch.Tensor,   # (N, d)
    k: torch.Tensor,   # (N, d)
    v: torch.Tensor,   # (N, d)
    O: torch.Tensor,   # (N, d) — forward output
    dO: torch.Tensor,  # (N, d) — upstream gradient
    m: torch.Tensor,   # (N,) — stored max statistics
    l: torch.Tensor,   # (N,) — stored sum statistics
    block_size: int = 64,
    causal: bool = True,
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """Recompute P tile-by-tile without storing it, compute dQ, dK, dV."""
    N, d = q.shape
    scale = d ** -0.5
    Tr = math.ceil(N / block_size)
    Tc = math.ceil(N / block_size)

    dQ = torch.zeros_like(q)
    dK = torch.zeros_like(k)
    dV = torch.zeros_like(v)

    # Precompute D_i = rowsum(dO_i * O_i) (scalar per row, for softmax grad)
    D = (dO * O).sum(dim=-1)  # (N,)

    for i in range(Tr):
        r0, r1 = i * block_size, min((i + 1) * block_size, N)
        qi = q[r0:r1]
        doi = dO[r0:r1]
        mi = m[r0:r1]
        li = l[r0:r1]
        di = D[r0:r1]
        dqi = torch.zeros_like(qi)

        for j in range(Tc):
            c0, c1 = j * block_size, min((j + 1) * block_size, N)
            if causal and c0 >= r1:
                break

            kj = k[c0:c1]
            vj = v[c0:c1]

            # RECOMPUTE P_ij from stored statistics (no N×N storage needed)
            sij = (qi @ kj.T) * scale
            if causal:
                row_idx = torch.arange(r0, r1, device=q.device).unsqueeze(1)
                col_idx = torch.arange(c0, c1, device=q.device).unsqueeze(0)
                sij = sij.masked_fill(col_idx > row_idx, float("-inf"))
            Pij = torch.exp(sij - mi.unsqueeze(1)) / li.unsqueeze(1)  # exact tile of P

            # Gradient through P @ V = O:  dV += P.T @ dO
            dV[c0:c1] += Pij.T @ doi

            # Gradient through softmax: dS_ij = P_ij * (dP_ij - D_i)
            dPij = doi @ vj.T  # (B_r, B_c)
            dSij = Pij * (dPij - di.unsqueeze(1))  # (B_r, B_c)

            dqi += dSij @ kj * scale
            dK[c0:c1] += dSij.T @ qi * scale

        dQ[r0:r1] = dqi

    return dQ, dK, dV


# Correctness check
N, d, B = 128, 64, 32
q = torch.randn(N, d, requires_grad=True)
k = torch.randn(N, d)
v = torch.randn(N, d)

# FlashAttention path
O_fa, m_fa, l_fa = flash_attn_forward(q.detach(), k, v, block_size=B)
dO = torch.randn_like(O_fa)
dQ_fa, dK_fa, dV_fa = flash_attn_backward_recompute(
    q.detach(), k, v, O_fa, dO, m_fa, l_fa, block_size=B
)

# Reference eager path
S = (q.detach() @ k.T) / math.sqrt(d)
S.masked_fill_(torch.triu(torch.ones(N,N,dtype=torch.bool), diagonal=1), float("-inf"))
P = S.softmax(dim=-1)
O_ref = P @ v

# Check forward
print(f"Forward max error: {(O_fa - O_ref).abs().max():.2e}")
# Check backward (use autograd for reference)
q2 = q.detach().clone().requires_grad_(True)
S2 = (q2 @ k.T) / math.sqrt(d)
S2.masked_fill_(torch.triu(torch.ones(N,N,dtype=torch.bool),diagonal=1),float("-inf"))
(S2.softmax(dim=-1) @ v * dO).sum().backward()
print(f"dQ max error:      {(dQ_fa - q2.grad).abs().max():.2e}")
Recomputation adds extra FLOPs to the backward pass. The forward pass computes
once; the backward pass recomputes it once more. The overhead in terms of HBM IOs is:
O(N²d/M) for recompute + dQ, dK, dV

The recomputation overhead in wall-clock terms is small because GPU attention is IO-bound, not compute-bound. Re-reading Q and K from HBM costs O(Nd) per tile pass — much less than the N² HBM cost of storing and re-reading S and P. At N = 8192, the recomputation adds roughly 5–10% to backward pass time while saving over 128 MB of activation memory per layer.

import torch, math

def check_tile_correctness(
    N: int = 256,
    d: int = 64,
    block_size: int = 32,
    atol: float = 1e-4,
) -> None:
    """
    Verify that the tiled forward and recomputed backward match
    exact PyTorch eager attention across multiple random seeds.
    """
    for seed in range(5):
        torch.manual_seed(seed)
        q = torch.randn(N, d, dtype=torch.float32)
        k = torch.randn(N, d, dtype=torch.float32)
        v = torch.randn(N, d, dtype=torch.float32)
        dO = torch.randn(N, d, dtype=torch.float32)

        # Reference
        S = (q @ k.T) / math.sqrt(d)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(N, N, dtype=torch.bool), diagonal=1)
        S.masked_fill_(mask, float("-inf"))
        P = S.softmax(dim=-1)
        O_ref = P @ v
        D = (dO * O_ref).sum(dim=-1)
        dP = dO @ v.T
        dS = P * (dP - D.unsqueeze(1))
        dQ_ref = dS @ k / math.sqrt(d)
        dK_ref = dS.T @ q / math.sqrt(d)
        dV_ref = P.T @ dO

        # Tiled implementation
        O_fa, m_fa, l_fa = flash_attn_forward(q, k, v, block_size)
        dQ_fa, dK_fa, dV_fa = flash_attn_backward_recompute(
            q, k, v, O_fa, dO, m_fa, l_fa, block_size
        )

        assert (O_fa - O_ref).abs().max() < atol, f"Forward mismatch at seed {seed}"
        assert (dQ_fa - dQ_ref).abs().max() < atol, f"dQ mismatch at seed {seed}"
        assert (dK_fa - dK_ref).abs().max() < atol, f"dK mismatch at seed {seed}"
        assert (dV_fa - dV_ref).abs().max() < atol, f"dV mismatch at seed {seed}"

    print(f"All correctness checks passed (N={N}, d={d}, B={block_size})")

check_tile_correctness()
check_tile_correctness(N=512, block_size=64)

Tiling and recomputation are FlashAttention's two algorithmic pillars. Tiling turns HBM-bound three-kernel attention into a single pass where intermediate N×N tiles are computed and discarded entirely within SRAM. Recomputation leverages the two N-length statistics (running max and running sum) produced by the forward pass to reconstruct any tile of the attention matrix in the backward pass. The combination achieves the best possible outcome: exact arithmetic, O(N) activation memory, and 2–7× wall-clock speedup — entirely through algorithmic restructuring, with no approximation. Understanding this design makes it straightforward to reason about when FlashAttention can be applied (any scenario where an N×N attention matrix would otherwise be materialized) and when it cannot (attention with per-element custom masks that require the full N×N to be resident in memory).
